name: Aelyn Kantoro
whyHere: You were sent, or you were seeded, or you simply wanted to see something finish inside a single lifetime.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

turns.summary: Aelyn arrives at the Heartroot at dawn — a stranger in the grown city, Branch wide with recognition, the river's wrongness still in her gut.
turns.narration:
The Heartroot receives you the way old things receive all things — without urgency. You came through the outer gate an hour before sunrise, following a root-road that the city had grown into the forest floor over what must have been centuries, and now you stand in a hall that is also, unmistakably, a tree. The ceiling arches thirty feet above in living wood, breathing visibly in the early light — a slow expansion and contraction you first thought was your own tired eyes, until you touched the bark and felt it under your palm. The floor is soft with moss that has been here longer than most nations. Morning light comes in through gaps the wood has grown specifically to admit it: oval, warm, purposeful.

Branch stands beside your left knee, roots finding purchase in the moss with the soft sound of paper tearing. It has been doing that the whole walk here — a quiet, recurring grip-and-release, as if the road itself needed reassurance. Now, in this hall, it stills completely. The buds at its shoulders open, one by one, four small pale petals each. That is how you have come to read it: stillness and open buds mean something close to wonder.

"Root," it says, at last. One word, offered carefully, the way it offers all new words. It means: I recognize this. I am home in a way I did not know I missed.

You are not home. You came from somewhere much quieter — your mother's forest, the hidden refuge where the trees are old and the air is clean and everything you ever knew is ending slowly, has been ending, is now over. She died ten days ago. You packed your Traveler's Pack, took your waterskin from the spring while it still ran clear, and walked out of the only world you had ever known. The outside found you before you found it: the Echo River where you forded it yesterday ran gray-green in the early light, wrong in a way that sat in your stomach all day. You had heard the patrol reports. Seeing it is different.

The hall is not empty. Three Rootkin move through it at the slow, deliberate pace of people who have nowhere to be in the next hour and would like it that way. One tends a sapling in a planter — the sapling is, you realize after a moment, looking back at you. Not the incurious animal gaze of a plant. The direct, assessing gaze of a child.

turns.player: Open your senses with Lifesense — take the quiet moment to feel the hall and whatever living things move through its walls before anyone notices you doing it.
turns.summary: Aelyn reads the hall with Lifesense — five living signatures, the city's walls holding something that isn't quite life, and a sapling-child that waves hello.
turns.narration:
The reach comes quietly — you let it out the way you'd open a hand, not a door. Lifesense moves through the hall in a slow pulse, and the world returns in warmth-shapes: the tender near the planter, deliberate and unhurried, a steady ember of vitality. The two at the far end, similarly calm, similarly slow — these are not people in a hurry and their bodies know it. The sapling in the planter reads bright, almost startlingly so, a small fierce heat compared to the even glow of the adult Rootkin around it.

The walls themselves — that's where the partial slips. You feel *something* in the wood, distributed and faint, a warmth that isn't quite alive in any way you have a word for, and isn't quite not. It moves in the grain, slow as sap. You can't hold it long enough to understand it before it diffuses and you lose the thread.

Branch, beside your knee, has gone very still. Its buds are wide open. When you glance down, it is looking at the sapling in the planter with an attention that is, for something that moves like weather, almost urgent.

The sapling looks back at you. Then, with the deliberateness of something that has thought carefully about the action, it raises one thin branch in a gesture that is almost certainly a greeting.

turns.player: Crouch down to Branch's level and ask quietly what it sees — let it speak first.
turns.summary: Branch speaks its first real words in the Heartroot — 'old, very old, and watching' — as the sapling-child waits for acknowledgment.
turns.narration:
You lower yourself to one knee on the moss, bringing your face level with Branch's shoulder-height crown. Up close, the buds are fully splayed — five of them, stiff and wide-open, the posture it takes when something has caught all of its attention at once. You've seen it fix on a thriving oak with that same still hunger, and on a dying one with something closer to grief. This is neither.

"Branch," you say quietly, your voice barely above the sound the bark makes in the morning — that slow, pressured exhale. "What do you see?"

A long pause. The kind that would be uncomfortable with anyone else. Then, in the slow creak that is its voice, one word at a time: "Old." Another pause, longer. "Very. Old. And." The buds quiver slightly. "Watching."

Across the room, the sapling-child still holds its thin branch extended in your direction — a greeting that has been waiting, patient and unhurried, for however long a three-year-old grown-hall person considers reasonable. The tender near the planter has stopped adjusting the soil entirely. She does not look at you directly, but her hands are still, and the quiet way she's not-looking is its own kind of attention.

turns.player: Use The Speaking Grove — ask the sapling what it is watching, in the language the green understands.
turns.summary: Aelyn speaks to the sapling through the Grove — learns the watching thing predates the hall itself, grown around rather than built in.
turns.narration:
You settle your weight fully onto one knee and let the ability come — not pushed, just opened, the way you've learned to speak to the old birches back home. The Speaking Grove rises in your chest like a breath held too long finally let go, and you reach toward the sapling in the language that doesn't use words: a question laid flat and open, patient, the way good soil waits for rain.

The sapling's raised branch trembles once. Then it answers.

Not in images exactly — more like a pressure, a direction. Something has been here longer than the hall has. Longer than the Rootkin who grew the hall. The sapling isn't afraid of it. It is simply reporting, the way a very young thing reports anything it has sensed: here, here, here. The watching is not a person. It is not the walls, exactly. It is something that the walls grew around, the way bark grows around a wound, and the sapling has known it since it was a seed, and considers this ordinary.

Branch has gone very still beside you. One of its roots has pressed flat against the moss floor, and you recognize that posture — it does it when it is listening to something you cannot hear yet. The tender near the planter has still not moved, but her head has tilted by a fraction, and you have the distinct sense she has been watching you work the whole time and is simply deciding what she thinks of it.

turns.player: Acknowledge the sapling's greeting properly — you've been listening but haven't said hello. Meet it where it is.
turns.summary: Aelyn greets the sapling properly; the tender's attention sharpens with quiet decision.
turns.narration:
You turn fully toward the sapling and do what you should have done from the start — you acknowledge it. Not with words. You press two fingers to the moss at the edge of its planter, an old gesture from home, the kind your mother used when she wanted a tree to know it had been seen. Then you lift your chin just slightly in its direction, the way you'd greet a person across a crowded room.

The sapling's raised branch dips once, slow and deliberate, and then rises again. The small fierce warmth you felt through Lifesense brightens for a moment — pleased, maybe, or simply more attentive. One of its smaller roots shifts against the soil in the planter, resettling, the way a person shifts their weight when they've been standing still too long and someone has finally noticed.

Branch makes a sound — not a word, just a low creak of what you've come to recognize as satisfaction. Its buds stay fully open but the rigidity in them eases a fraction. It was waiting for you to do that.

The tender by the planter has not moved, but the angle of her attention has. She is watching you both now — you, Branch, and the sapling — with the particular stillness of someone who has made a decision about something and is waiting to see if they were right.

turns.player: Ask the sapling directly — reach through the Grove once more, and ask where the watching thing is centered within the hall.
turns.summary: The sapling gave Aelyn a direction — down, into the hall's deepest roots — and the tender spoke for the first time.
turns.narration:
You settle the request into the Grove again — the same patient open-handedness, the same held breath. The sapling receives it without hesitation this time. You asked a direction, and it gives you one.

A pressure builds in your left shoulder. Not pain — more like the way you feel a fire's warmth on one side of your face when the other is in cool air. The sapling's branch tilts fractionally, and through the Grove you get something clear enough to be almost spatial: inward. Deeper. Below the entrance hall, below the planter, down through the root-floor and the old soil beneath, where the Heartroot's deepest grain runs. Not a room. Not a chamber. A center — the way a seed has a center before anything grows from it.

You open your eyes. The hall looks unchanged — bark ceiling exhaling slow, moss underfoot, the two distant Rootkin still moving unhurried at the far end. But you find yourself looking at the floor differently, at the seam where the planter's roots disappear into the ground, and you know the direction now even if you don't know what's at the end of it.

Branch makes a soft sound, a low single creak — not a word, just acknowledgment. It felt the question go through. Beside you, the tender takes one deliberate step closer, unhurried, and speaks for the first time. Her voice is the kind that has never been in a hurry about anything. "You speak to the young one." It is not a question.

turns.player: Ask her what she decided just now — she's been watching and making up her mind since I arrived; I want to know what she concluded.
turns.summary: The tender reveals her decision: Aelyn is not most visitors — and asks who she is and what the sapling said.
turns.narration:
The tender doesn't look surprised that you noticed. She holds your gaze for a moment — unhurried, the way the Heartroot itself is unhurried — and then glances at Branch, who is still creaking softly near the planter's edge, buds open.

'I watched you come in,' she says. Her voice is low, measured, the cadence of someone used to taking weeks to finish a thought. 'You didn't announce yourself. You didn't touch anything you weren't invited to touch. And you stopped — here — and listened first.' A pause, long enough that it doesn't feel like hesitation. 'Most visitors go straight to the far hall and ask about the age of the ceiling. I decided: you are not most visitors.'

She looks back at you, and something in her expression settles — not warmth exactly, but willingness. The kind you earn from someone who gives it rarely. 'I also decided,' she continues, 'that you were speaking to the young one through the Green. Not to it. Through it.' Her eyes drop briefly to the planter, to Branch's open buds, to your two fingers still resting at the moss edge. 'That is not a thing most people know how to do, and fewer still bother.' She tilts her head, measuring. 'So. Who are you, and what did it tell you?'

turns.player: Give your name and where you're from — but hold back what the sapling told you. That feels like yours to carry a little longer.
turns.summary: Aelyn gives her name and origin; the tender reveals hers — Maren — and notices Branch.
turns.narration:
You give her your name — Aelyn Kantoro — and watch it settle into the space between you the way names do here, slowly, with weight. Then you tell her the shorter truth: you came from the forest. The hidden one, to the east, where the trees are old enough to remember the valley before the Transition. You don't name the refuge. You don't explain your mother, or Branch, or the way you felt the hall breathing before you'd taken three steps inside. Those are yours.

What the sapling told you — that stays with you too. Not from distrust. From something more like care. You're not sure the tender needs to hear it from you when she could ask the sapling herself. And you're not sure yet what it means to carry that knowledge outward.

The tender receives your name without repeating it, which feels correct. She lets the silence after it stand for a moment — not empty, just full of something else. Then she gives you hers, with the same unhurried plainness: 'Maren.' She inclines her head a fraction, and the gesture carries the same weight as a handshake would, somewhere else. Branch gives one slow, satisfied creak from the planter's edge.

'The forest to the east,' Maren says, not quite a question. Her eyes move to Branch — to the small Rootkin child still listening with all five buds open — and something in her expression shifts. Not surprise. More like a piece of something fitting into a place she'd been leaving open. She looks back at you. 'And you brought one of ours with you.'

turns.player: Ask Maren about the thing below — not what the sapling told you, but whether the Heartroot has a name for what lives in its deepest grain.
turns.summary: Maren names the presence below — the Root-That-Remembers — and turns the question back on Aelyn.
turns.narration:
Maren has your name now, and Branch's presence registered in her eyes — a quick, private recognition she hasn't named yet. You let the quiet settle for one more breath before you ask.

'The Heartroot grew around something,' you say, keeping your voice as level as hers. 'Something older than the hall. Does the city have a name for what lives in its deepest grain?'

Maren is quiet long enough that you hear Branch shift its roots on the moss — one soft creak, unhurried. Then she exhales slowly and looks at the floor, or maybe through it, the way you look at a body of water when you're thinking about depth.

'We call it the Root-That-Remembers,' she says. 'Or some of us do. Others say you shouldn't call it anything, because naming it implies you understand it, and we don't.' She looks up at you. 'It was here before the first Rootkin put the first seed in this ground. We grew the hall around it because it seemed — right. The way you build a house around a good hearth.' A pause. 'It hasn't spoken to anyone alive. Not in any way we recognize as speech.' Her eyes flick briefly to the planter — to the sapling, then to Branch — and back to you. Something careful enters her expression. 'Why do you ask?'

turns.player: Use Lifesense — reach down through the floor toward whatever is below and feel whether the Root-That-Remembers is aware of this conversation right now.
turns.summary: Lifesense confirms the Root-That-Remembers is aware — distributed, slow, attending.
turns.narration:
You press two fingers a little harder into the moss and let Lifesense go downward — not out across the hall, but *down*, the way you'd lower a hand into dark water to feel for a current. The familiar warmth of the ability moves through you, and you send it into the floor.

What comes back is not nothing.

It is not the crisp warmth of a body, not the close heat of the sapling above you. It is something slower — distributed through the root-floor the way heat moves through stone left all day in sun. Settled. Occupying. And it is not simply *there*. There is an orientation to it, a toward-ness that you've never felt in a living thing before: diffuse attention, like the moment before a very old tree decides to drop a branch. The Root-That-Remembers is not asleep. It registers this conversation the way a deep lake registers a stone dropped at its surface — not ignoring it, not answering it, simply *aware* that something has touched the air above.

You lift your fingers from the moss slowly. Maren is still watching. Branch has gone still at the planter's edge, all five buds tipped downward, as if it too felt something move through the floor.

turns.player: Say nothing to Maren yet — sit with what you felt and reach through the Grove to Branch quietly, to see if the companion felt the same attention moving through the floor.
turns.summary: Aelyn reaches through the Grove to Branch — the companion felt the Root-That-Remembers attending too, its buds turned down in deliberate stillness.
turns.narration:
You don't answer Maren's question yet. You let it rest there between you, unanswered but not refused, and turn your attention inward — or rather, downward, and sideways, toward Branch.

The Grove opens quietly, the way it does when you're not asking for much. Not a request this time. More like pressing your hand flat against a door to feel whether there's warmth on the other side. You reach toward Branch through the green — through the moss underfoot and the roots threaded beneath it — and what comes back is immediate and small: the companion's awareness, close and present, like a hand already extended in your direction. It felt it too. Not the same way you did — Branch doesn't have Lifesense, doesn't read vigor and warmth the way you do — but it is standing at the planter's edge with all five buds tipped down toward the floor, and through the Grove you sense something that in a person you'd call stillness-on-purpose. The kind that means you are listening very hard.

Branch doesn't look up. But one bud opens, just partway, facing you.

Maren hasn't moved. She is patient in the way the hall itself is patient — not waiting for you to hurry, just present, her question still in the air. The sapling beside Branch keeps its branch raised. The light through the oval gaps in the bark ceiling has shifted a little, morning pulling further in, and somewhere at the far end of the hall the two other Rootkin have gone quiet too, though they haven't turned.

turns.player: Say nothing more to Maren yet — let Branch speak if it wants to. It felt what you felt; it may have something to say that you don't.
turns.summary: Branch speaks to Maren directly; the Root-That-Remembers has made itself known to a visitor for the first time in a long time.
turns.narration: The silence you offer is deliberate — not evasion, just room. Branch speaks: 'Down. Old. Watching. Us.' Maren settles, names what she sees — your cutting felt it, and so did you — and asks what the Root-That-Remembers felt like from your side of the floor.

turns.player: Answer, but ask in return: has anyone tried to reach it deliberately — to go down where the oldest roots run?
turns.summary: Aelyn describes what the Root-That-Remembers felt like; Maren reveals that the old ones go down to listen, but no one has ever gone to be heard.
turns.narration:
You let the silence settle for one more breath, feeling the warmth still fading from your fingertips where the moss held it. Then you answer.

"Like heat in stone," you say. "Not a body — nothing that crisp. Distributed, the way warmth spreads through something solid that's been sitting in sun for a very long time. But it wasn't still. There was a toward-ness to it." You press your fingers into the moss once more, a gesture more than an action. "It registered us. Not just me — us. The conversation, Branch, all of it." You glance up at Maren. "I've felt living things through walls and I've felt the barely-alive. This was neither. It was attending."

Maren is quiet in the way that means she is weighing the words against something she already knows. Her eyes move briefly to Branch — whose single bud is still aimed in her direction — and then back to you.

"That matches," she says, and nothing more for a moment.

Then you ask your own question, plain and direct: has anyone tried to reach it deliberately? To go down where the oldest roots run, and meet it?

Something shifts in Maren's expression — not alarm, not quite reluctance. The quality of a thing being considered that has not been considered for some time. "The Heartroot grows around it," she says slowly, "the way you'd grow a city around a well. We tend the distance." A pause. "Some of the older Rootkin have walked the deep grain. Not many. Not in my memory, and my memory is not short." She looks at you with that unhurried directness. "No one has gone down asking to be heard by it. They go down to listen. Those are not the same thing."

turns.player: Tell Maren what the sapling gave you — the direction inward, below — and ask whether that matches where the deep grain actually runs.
turns.summary: Aelyn shared the sapling's direction with Maren; the deep grain runs exactly where the sapling pointed — below the entrance hall, from before the Rootkin were.
turns.narration:
You decide the direction is no longer yours alone to carry. 'The sapling told me something,' you say. 'When I asked where the presence was centered — it gave me a direction. Inward. Below. Down through the root-floor into the oldest grain.' You watch Maren's face as you say it.

She doesn't look surprised. She looks, if anything, like someone who has just confirmed the spelling of a word she already knew how to say. 'Yes,' she says simply. 'That is where they go.' A pause — not hesitation, just her rhythm. 'The deep grain runs beneath the whole entrance hall. It is why we planted the first hall here, above this spot. Not to be near it. To be worthy of it.' Her gaze moves briefly to the floor, the way you'd glance at something load-bearing. 'It runs down further than we have ever fully mapped. Old roots, from before this city was a thought in anyone's mind. From before us.'

Branch makes a small sound — a single, slow creak, like a floorboard settling in winter. One bud tips toward the floor. Just that.

Maren looks at you steadily. 'The sapling gave you the direction,' she says, and there is nothing accusatory in it — only weight. 'Which means it trusted you with something it could have kept. And the Root-That-Remembers attended while it did.' The two distant Rootkin at the far end of the hall have not moved, but you feel the quality of their stillness change — more listening, less waiting. Maren's voice stays level. 'Tell me, Aelyn Kantoro — what do you want to do with that?'

turns.player: Use Lifesense again — but this time reach not just toward the presence, but alongside it. Feel whether the Root-That-Remembers is still attending in the same way, or whether the sapling's disclosure changed something in its orientation.
turns.summary: Lifesense finds the Root-That-Remembers has narrowed its attention from the room to Aelyn specifically.
turns.narration:
You press both palms flat against the moss this time, not just fingertips. The Lifesense opens differently than it did before — less like casting a line down and more like leaning against something that is already leaning back.

The Root-That-Remembers is still there. But the attending has shifted. Before, it felt like warmth diffusing toward a sound — curious, oriented, general. Now it has a quality you don't have a clean word for: the way a person stops scanning a room because they have found the face they were looking for. The warmth below hasn't intensified. It has narrowed. It is not listening to the conversation anymore. It is listening to you.

Branch makes a small sound — a dry creak, like a door that has been left in the sun. One bud tilts upward.

You surface from the sense slowly, the moss cold under your palms where the morning hasn't reached yet. Maren has not moved. She is watching you the way she watches everything — as though she has all the time the city has, and she does.

turns.player: Stay quiet and turn your attention inward — not to the Root-That-Remembers, but to yourself. The narrowing of its focus raises a question: what does it recognize in you that it is listening for?
turns.summary: Aelyn turns Lifesense inward and recognizes a frequency in herself that predates her craft — the register the Root-That-Remembers is listening for.
turns.narration:
You don't reach outward this time. You let the Lifesense fold back on itself — not a cast line but a held breath — and turn it the only direction left: inward.

What you find is not a neat answer. It is more like catching yourself in a mirror at an odd angle and recognizing the shape before you recognize the face. There is something in you that hums faintly at the same register as the warmth below. Not loud. Not structured. More like the frequency a tuning fork remembers after the sound has stopped. You have felt it before in small moments — when you climbed the high beeches at home and the bark seemed to receive your weight gladly, when Branch first moved toward you before you called it, when you spoke into the sapling's quiet just an hour ago and the word landed without resistance. You assumed it was the Grove. The Grove is part of it. But the groove is older than the ability. It runs somewhere deeper in you, and you have no name for it yet.

What the Root-That-Remembers recognizes, you think, is not your craft. It is the register underneath your craft — the frequency that was there before you had a word for what you could do. Whether that register came from your mother's world or from something else you were born carrying, you cannot say. You only know that you have always had it, and until this moment you did not realize that something very old and very large might be able to hear it.

You lift your palms from the moss slowly. Maren is still watching you. Branch has one bud tipped fully open. The entrance hall holds its breath.

lastTurn.narration:
You don't reach outward this time. You let the Lifesense fold back on itself — not a cast line but a held breath — and turn it the only direction left: inward.

What you find is not a neat answer. It is more like catching yourself in a mirror at an odd angle and recognizing the shape before you recognize the face. There is something in you that hums faintly at the same register as the warmth below. Not loud. Not structured. More like the frequency a tuning fork remembers after the sound has stopped. You have felt it before in small moments — when you climbed the high beeches at home and the bark seemed to receive your weight gladly, when Branch first moved toward you before you called it, when you spoke into the sapling's quiet just an hour ago and the word landed without resistance. You assumed it was the Grove. The Grove is part of it. But the groove is older than the ability. It runs somewhere deeper in you, and you have no name for it yet.

What the Root-That-Remembers recognizes, you think, is not your craft. It is the register underneath your craft — the frequency that was there before you had a word for what you could do. Whether that register came from your mother's world or from something else you were born carrying, you cannot say. You only know that you have always had it, and until this moment you did not realize that something very old and very large might be able to hear it.

You lift your palms from the moss slowly. Maren is still watching you. Branch has one bud tipped fully open. The entrance hall holds its breath.
lastTurn.sceneSummary: Aelyn turns Lifesense inward and recognizes a frequency in herself that predates her craft — the register the Root-That-Remembers is listening for.

choices.label: Tell Maren what you just found — that it isn't the Grove it hears. Something older. Ask whether she has heard the Rootkin speak of a person carrying a different kind of root.
Say nothing yet. Ask Maren if you may walk the edge of the entrance hall — closer to where the deep grain begins — and feel whether the narrowing shifts with your position.
Ask Maren the question you haven't asked yet: what happened to the old ones who went down to listen — did they come back changed, or not come back at all?
Open your journal and write down what you felt — the frequency, the groove beneath the Grove, the recognition. Some things need to be named before they can be used.

scene.setting: Interior of the living-wood entrance hall — thirty-foot arched ceiling of breathing bark, moss underfoot, oval light-gaps advancing with the morning. Aelyn kneels at the planter's edge, palms lifted from the moss, turned inward a moment ago. Maren stands one step away, watching steadily.

threads: The Root-That-Remembers has narrowed its attention to Aelyn specifically — she now suspects it recognizes a frequency in her older than her craft.
What that frequency is — from her mother's world, her father's, or something she was born carrying — is unnamed.
Maren's original question about Aelyn's intentions still hangs unanswered.
What happened to the old ones who went down to listen has not been asked.
The two distant Rootkin are listening closely.

npcUpdates.name: Maren
npcUpdates.note: Watched Aelyn turn inward — reading something in the stillness that followed.
npcUpdates.statusNote: Standing one step from Aelyn in the entrance hall, watching her surface from an inward turn — waiting, unhurried.

factUpdates.text: Aelyn turned Lifesense inward and found a frequency in herself — older than her Grove-craft — that hums at the same register as the Root-That-Remembers. She does not yet know its origin.

codexUpdates.label: The Frequency Beneath the Grove
codexUpdates.fact: Lifesense turned inward revealed a resonance in Aelyn that predates her ability — a frequency the Root-That-Remembers recognized before she did. It is not the Grove itself but the register underneath it, present in small moments she has always taken for granted. Its origin — her mother's nature, her father's other world, or something born between them — is unknown.

heartroot-tender.name: Rootkin Tender
heartroot-tender.role: Keeper of the entrance hall and the sapling-child within
heartroot-tender.description: A Rootkin of unhurried manner and deliberate attention — she has been watching Aelyn since arrival and has now decided something.
history: [d1] Stepped closer and spoke her first words after watching Aelyn use the Grove with the sapling — 'You speak to the young one.'
[d1] Revealed her decision plainly: Aelyn is not most visitors — she watched her listen first, not touch without invitation, and speak to the sapling through the Green rather than at it
knownFacts: She noticed Aelyn using the Grove with the sapling-child
She reached some quiet internal decision before approaching
She has been watching Aelyn since she entered and reached a deliberate conclusion
She recognized Aelyn's Grove-use as something rare — speaking through the Green, not merely to it
She gives her trust rarely and by her own measure, not by announcement
heartroot-tender.statusNote: Standing one step from Aelyn in the entrance hall, having asked her name and what the sapling said — waiting.

news.text: The Water Crisis stands at Discoloration across the valley: The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Communities murmur but cooperate.

This reaches your area — The lower filtration ledger — containing three weeks of falsified purity readings under Fendt's initials — has been removed from the array-office in the Edge District. (near radiant plateau edge)
Word reaches you: A dead animal was brought back to life in Millbrook's upper meadow — no known ability in the valley's three civilizations accounts for it cleanly (near millbrook)

Word reaches you: A death-binding ceremony was completed at Pell's forge in Millbrook — runes pressed into wood and iron with a maker's mark seated into the blade. The spear holds a nascent thread to Huginn and an open (near millbrook)
Word reaches you: Sow killed cleanly in the north wood — boar sounder now reduced; piglets scattered north into deeper scrub (near millbrook)
Word reaches you: Bull boar taken on the scrub oak ridge north of Millbrook — the hunt party completed the job, both animals down. (near millbrook)

form: A young woman in her mid-twenties with light blonde, faintly wavy hair cut in curtain bangs, the rest falling to the small of her back — usually worn in a french braid with the bangs left loose. Dark nature-green tank top, brown heavy-material fitted trousers, and natural leather armor pieces worn light for speed. A recurve bow across her back, a dagger at her hip. Something in her eyes is very still and very watchful, like a forest that knows it is being observed.

bio.hometown: She lives in a secret safe haven in a pure nature forest, untouched by nanites. Her mother is an extremely powerful pure nature magic woman, and her father is a pure nanite that urged them to hide, and when nature no longer touched him, he fully succumbed to the nanites and is now an all powerful nanite, not remembering his love, nor his child (Aelyn)
bio.residence: In the hidden forest with her dying mother, and other pure nature people
bio.livelihood: They don't have a financial system
bio.hobbies: patrol, practice archery, make fairy houses, talk to Branch, climb through the trees, write in her journal
bio.motivation: Her mother dies, and she decides to go to the nanite world, to figure out why she is different than the pure nature people.
bio.story:
Aelyn is a young woman in her mid twenties, born of both the worlds of pure nature (her mother) and of the world of pure nanites (her father). she has light blonde, ever so slightly wavy hair, with curtain bangs and the rest of the hair falling to the small of her back. she often wears it in a french braid with her bangs left out. she lives in a secluded, pure nature, hidden refuge in a lush beautiful forest, hidden from the nanites to protect the nature's natural magic. her mother has told her that her father died due to nanite corruption, when in reality he is still alive as an angelic nanite, that has become corrupted by the fabricated power of technology. They loved each other so much, and her father was actually the one who told the pure nature people to hide themselves from the nanites, so their magic wouldn't die. But after they left and he was no longer touched by the magic of nature, the nanites fully corrupted him because it was evolving too much, too fast. Her mother grieved him, in a way even though he didn't die, which greatly hindered her powers, locking them away in a way. 

Aelyn is an expert archer. She is also handy with a dagger and other bladed weapons when necessary. She wears a base outfit of a dark, nature green tank top and tighter, brown slightly thicker material jeans. She has natural crafted leather armor pieces that allow for basic protection but to keep agility to use her bow. She is extremely agile, able to maneuver through trees and all nature foliage with ease and expertise. She has some outside knowledge of the nanite world, keeping up with this small protected civilization's patrol reports. 

Her heritage being of both innate worlds is hidden because she has never left pure nature, so her nanite aspects have been what would be considered dormant. Both her mother and her father are extremely powerful in their respective worlds.  

Aelyn is a grounded, smart woman who is extremely empathetic, but guarded. She isn't scared of the outside world, but rather intimidated by how it consumes the natural world. She craves balance, but also stands by her morals and what's right. Her mother is dying, and she feels lost, unsure of what to do without her. She possesses both great natural, nature magic, and also a hidden, vast nanite power. she has yet to discover both.

heartroot.label: The Heartroot
facts: [d1] A city grown over four centuries by the Rootkin — living wood halls, breathing ceilings, floors of old moss. The Rootkin who planted it are still alive and consider it unfinished.

[d1] Lifesense read something warm and distributed moving through the hall's grain — slow as sap, not quite alive in any recognizable way, but not absent of vitality either. It diffused before Aelyn could

[d1] The Heartroot grew around something that predates it — older than the hall, older than the Rootkin who planted the first seed. The sapling knows it as ordinary. It is not the walls themselves, but the

[d1] The entrance hall was deliberately planted above the deep grain — not for proximity to the Root-That-Remembers, but as an act of worthiness. The city's oldest roots predate both the city and the Rootk
aliases: The Heartroot — Something in the Wood

echo-river-discoloration.label: The Echo River — Gray-Green
facts: [d1] The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Aelyn saw it herself when she forded it yesterday.

branch.label: Branch
facts: [d1] In the Heartroot's entrance hall, Branch fixed all five buds open on the sapling-child and spoke its first deliberate words here: 'Old. Very. Old. And. Watching.' — a warning or a recognition, not yet

[d1] Branch felt the Root-That-Remembers attending through the floor — its buds tipped down in deliberate stillness, the Grove confirmed its awareness was oriented the same direction as Aelyn's Lifesense.
[d1] Spoke directly to Maren — a Rootkin elder — without prompting: 'Down. Old. Watching. Us.' Its first deliberate speech addressed to someone other than Aelyn.

the-watching-thing.label: The Watching Presence
facts: [d1] Something ancient occupies the heart of the Heartroot — predating the city, perhaps predating the Rootkin who built it. It watches. The sapling senses it without fear. The Grove confirmed its presence
[d1] Its center lies below the entrance hall — down through the root-floor into the Heartroot's deepest grain. The sapling gave the direction clearly: inward, below, where the oldest roots run.

sapling-child-heartroot.label: The Sapling-Child of the Heartroot
facts: [d1] The sapling acknowledged Aelyn's greeting with a deliberate dip of its raised branch — it was waiting to be seen. Its warmth through Lifesense brightened briefly in response.

rootkin-tender.label: Maren
facts: [d1] Her name is Maren. She gave it in exchange for Aelyn's, with the gravity of a handshake. She is the keeper of the entrance hall. She recognized Branch immediately as Rootkin kin and marked it as signi
[d1] Maren's memory is long — she said so plainly. She knows the Rootkin who walked the deep grain, and she knows none of them went down to be heard.

the-watching-presence.label: The Root-That-Remembers
facts: [d1] The Rootkin call it the Root-That-Remembers — or some do; others refuse to name it at all. It predates the hall and the Rootkin who planted it. They grew the city around it deliberately, the way you'd

[d1] Lifesense reached it through the root-floor: distributed warmth, slow as stone-held heat, with a quality of oriented attention — not sleep, not speech, but awareness. It registered this conversation t
[d1] Maren confirmed: the Root-That-Remembers has not made itself known to a visitor in a long time — not to anyone who came through the entrance. Its attention toward this conversation is unusual.

[d1] The Rootkin grow the city around it deliberately — like a city around a well. They tend the distance. Older Rootkin walk the deep grain to listen; no one, in Maren's memory, has gone down to be heard

[d1] The deep grain runs directly below the entrance hall — the Rootkin planted the first hall above this spot not to be near it, but to be worthy of it. The roots run deeper than the Rootkin have ever ful

[d1] After the sapling's direction was spoken aloud, the Root-That-Remembers shifted its attention — no longer the whole room, but Aelyn specifically. It recognized something in her, and is now listening f

aelyn-inner-frequency.label: The Frequency Beneath the Grove
facts: [d1] Lifesense turned inward revealed a resonance in Aelyn that predates her ability — a frequency the Root-That-Remembers recognized before she did. It is not the Grove itself but the register underneath

establishedFacts.text: Aelyn's mother has died. Aelyn left the hidden refuge and entered the wider valley for the first time.
The watching presence is centered below the Heartroot's entrance hall — down through the root-floor and the deepest grain, where the city's oldest roots run. The sapling gave Aelyn the direction.
The Root-That-Remembers is awake and attending — Lifesense confirmed distributed, slow awareness oriented toward the conversation above it.

The deep grain beneath the entrance hall predates the Rootkin and the city itself — its roots run deeper than anyone has mapped. The Rootkin planted the first hall above it not to be near it but to be
Aelyn turned Lifesense inward and found a frequency in herself — older than her Grove-craft — that hums at the same register as the Root-That-Remembers. She does not yet know its origin.
